title: Orange drizzle cake
subtitle: Loaf sponge soaked with orange syrup while warm
excerpt: Orange drizzle cake is a loaf sponge made with orange zest and saturated while still hot with a sharp orange juice and sugar syrup. The syrup soaks through the warm crumb and sets into a slightly crunchy surface as it cools. It is best eaten the next day, when the flavour has deepened throughout.
sourceNotes: The drizzle cake method of soaking a warm sponge with a citrus syrup is a British technique closely associated with lemon drizzle cake but equally suited to orange. The formula follows the standard British loaf sponge as documented in domestic baking manuals throughout the twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze un-drizzled; drizzle after defrosting for the best texture.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The flavour improves after 24 hours. Store wrapped at room temperature for up to 4 days.

content.text: Orange drizzle cake is a loaf sponge heavily flavoured with orange zest, baked until just done, then pierced all over and saturated with a sharp orange syrup while still hot. The syrup penetrates the warm crumb and sets as the cake cools, leaving a slightly crystallised surface and a moist, intensely flavoured interior. The cake is better on day two.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease a 900 g loaf tin and line it with baking paper. Beat the butter and caster sugar together until pale and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well between each. Fold in the flour and orange zest until just combined.

Pour into the lined tin and bake for 45 to 50 minutes. The cake is done when it is golden brown, has cracked along the top in a long split, springs back when pressed in the centre, and a skewer comes out clean. Do not take the cake from the tin yet.

While the cake is in the oven, stir the orange juice and granulated sugar together; do not try to dissolve the sugar. The granulated sugar gives the surface its slight crunch. As soon as the cake comes out of the oven, use a skewer to poke 20 to 30 holes across the top, going all the way through. Pour the drizzle slowly over the hot cake, allowing it to soak in between pourings. Leave in the tin until completely cool before removing.
